description: Interior wall view of a MODEST NEIGHBORHOOD HALL where a humble first-tier brocante is held — a community room, a parish hall, a village association space. The viewer faces the wall straight on (eye-level, no angle). The composition is dominated by the WALL (about 76 % of the image height in the middle), with a horizontal strip of FLOOR at the bottom (about 12 % of the image height) and a horizontal strip of CEILING at the top (about 12 % of the image height). The wall surface itself is plain and almost entirely EMPTY — no paintings, no frames, no posters drawn into the image (paintings will be composited on top later by the layout). Wall: faded cream-and-rose striped wallpaper, slightly peeling at the seams, a few small scuffs and old picture-hook holes (no actual paintings). Floor: scratched lacquered light-oak parquet, simple rectangular planks. Ceiling: a plain stuccoed beige finish with a small modest plaster cornice. Subtle architectural details only: a simple painted skirting board at the floor line, a discreet picture rail running along the top of the wall. Lighting: gentle warm overhead daylight, no harsh shadows. The wall area between the picture rail and the skirting must be left visually plain and uncluttered so that frames can be hung on it later.

Same wall view as the reference, but for a REFINED VINTAGE COVERED MARKET HALL — halles-style with industrial elegance. Replace the WALL material with warm exposed RED BRICK + subtle whitewashed mortar lines, NO horizontal bar, NO picture rail, NO copper trim, NO chair rail — strictly plain brick wall edge to edge between the floor and the ceiling cornice. Replace the FLOOR with polished honey-toned hardwood, wide planks. Replace the CEILING / cornice with an ornate cast-iron molding running horizontally. Lighting slightly warmer / more golden than the reference, evoking gas-lamp sconces just out of frame. Keep the same camera, same horizon, same proportions — only the materials change. The bulk of the wall must remain visually empty and uninterrupted.

Same wall view as the reference, but for a REFINED ART GALLERY / Drouot-style auction salon. Replace the WALL with smooth pale GREEN-GREY plaster, IMPECCABLE and freshly painted — absolutely no scuffs, no peeling, no picture-hook holes, no marks, no patina, no wear of any kind. The wall surface must read as pristine, museum-grade, perfectly smooth. Add a thin Greek-key gilded border just below the ceiling cornice. Replace the FLOOR with REFINED MARQUETRY: an elegant geometric inlaid wood pattern (multiple wood tones — walnut, ebony, satinwood — arranged in a star or rosette pattern), polished to a mirror finish, with a thin brass strip along the edge. Replace the CEILING / cornice with a refined classical moulded cornice, slightly gilded at the highlights. Lighting cooler and more diffused, museum-grade overhead light. Keep the same camera, same horizon, same proportions — only the materials and details change. The bulk of the wall must remain visually empty.

Same wall view as the reference, but for THE GRAND SALON DES ANTIQUAIRES DE DROUOT — the most prestigious auction salon. Replace the WALL with deep CRIMSON-RED DAMASK WALLPAPER with subtle gold-leaf floral flock pattern, plus a richly gilded moulded chair rail at mid-height (chair rail does not invade the central area). Replace the FLOOR with polished CREAMY MARBLE, thin grey veining, mirror-like. Replace the CEILING / cornice with an extremely ornate GILDED CROWN MOULDING and a hint of crystal chandelier crown barely entering the frame from above. Lighting: warm golden chandelier light, soft reflections on the marble. Keep the same camera, same horizon, same proportions — only the materials and ornamentation change. The bulk of the central wall area must remain visually empty.
